citations.narrative:
The metal surfaces that make up the entrances to the indoor housing for the capuchins have areas of excessive rust
where the animals can come in contact with it. These surfaces have evidence of pitting and flaking rust and one entrance
has a section that has rusted away, leaving a jagged edge.
Rust can compromise structural strength, present roughened and jagged surfaces where it is possible for the nonhuman
primates to injure themselves, and prevent proper cleaning and sanitization of a surface. Any surfaces that come in
contact with the nonhuman primates must be free of rust or damage that affects the structural integrity of the surface or
prevents cleaning.
To be corrected by March 11, 2026.

One male fox dug out of its enclosure on December 16, 2024 and was not recovered. According to a facility
representative, the edge of the dig barrier did not extend to meet the edge of the enclosure wall. This left a gap which
allowed the fox to dig out and escape. The facility attempted to trap the animal over a period of several months but these
attempts were unsuccessful. The facility has since changed the flooring of the enclosure to prevent any other foxes from
escaping.
Enclosures that are not constructed in a manner suitable for the species involved, including animals that dig, can result in
escape. The facility must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved.
The indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the
animals from injury and to contain the animals.
To be corrected from this day, February 20, 2026, forward.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee and facility representatives.
n

narrative:
3.75(c)(1)(i) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
The metal surfaces that make up the entrances to the indoor housing for the capuchins have areas of excessive rust 
where the animals can come in contact with it. These surfaces have evidence of pitting and flaking rust and one entrance 
has a section that has rusted away, leaving a jagged edge. 
Rust can compromise structural strength, present roughened and jagged surfaces where it is possible for the nonhuman 
primates to injure themselves, and prevent proper cleaning and sanitization of a surface. Any surfaces that come in 
contact with the nonhuman primates must be free of rust or damage that affects the structural integrity of the surface or 
prevents cleaning. 
To be corrected by March 11, 2026. 
3.125(a)  Critical
   
Facilities, general. 
One male fox dug out of its enclosure on December 16, 2024 and was not recovered. According to a facility 
representative, the edge of the dig barrier did not extend to meet the edge of the enclosure wall. This left a gap which 
allowed the fox to dig out and escape. The facility attempted to trap the animal over a period of several months but these 
attempts were unsuccessful. The facility has since changed the flooring of the enclosure to prevent any other foxes from 
escaping.  
Enclosures that are not constructed in a manner suitable for the species involved, including animals that dig, can result in 
escape. The facility must be constructed of such material and of such strength as appropriate for the animals involved. 
The indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the 
animals from injury and to contain the animals.  
To be corrected from this day, February 20, 2026, forward. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee and facility representatives.
